entry.title: CAP Heaven? Not Yet — But You Can See It from Here
entry.content:
I've been building things on the server side for a long time. I started with Java, spent years as a solution architect on Azure, and served as product owner for an AWS-based product. Somewhere along the way, I also became deeply embedded in the SAP world — ABAP, Fiori, RAP, the whole ecosystem.

So when I first looked at CAP I wasn't seeing it with fresh eyes. I was seeing it through the lens of someone who'd already lived through the cloud-native revolution on other platforms. Someone who knew what good looked like, and who'd felt the pain of what didn't work.

And CAP impressed me. Not because it's perfect, but because it's making the right bets.

Why does this matter now?

More customers are moving to clean core strategies, and side-by-side extensibility is no longer optional — it's becoming the default approach. At the same time, SAP is finally aligning with mainstream cloud-native patterns in a way that feels genuine rather than cosmetic. If you're involved in SAP development today, CAP is where these threads converge.

An Opinionated Framework — With Good Opinions

CAP is opinionated. And I mean that as the highest compliment.

In software development, "opinionated" sometimes gets thrown around like an insult. But anyone who's spent time in the SAP ecosystem knows the cost of unopinionated — the wild west of custom code where every team invents their own patterns, their own naming conventions, their own architectural mistakes. I've inherited enough legacy code to know that freedom without guidance is just chaos with extra steps.

CAP has opinions about how you model your data (CDS), how you define your services, how you handle persistence, and how you deal with events. And they're good opinions. They're the kind of opinions that save you from yourself at 2 AM when you're tempted to take a shortcut that your future self will curse.

The CDS-first approach forces you to think about your domain model before you start coding behavior. The service layer gives you a clean separation between what you expose and how things work internally. The event-driven model nudges you toward loosely coupled designs. None of this is revolutionary — I've lived with these patterns in Spring Boot for years, and the Rails community figured this out ages ago — but for the SAP ecosystem, it feels like a breath of fresh air.

Coming from Java and Node.js, CAP's conventions feel instantly familiar. Convention over configuration. Sensible defaults. Guardrails that keep you on the happy path. The difference is that CAP brings this philosophy to a world that desperately needs it — a world where too many projects have paid the price of building without shared conventions.

A typical example: say you're building a side-by-side extension that enriches sales orders with external credit scoring data. In CAP, you define your domain model in CDS, project a service on top, register an event handler that calls the external scoring API on each read, and you're done. The framework handles the OData exposure, the error handling patterns, and the integration with SAP's event mesh. You focus on the business logic, not the plumbing. That's what good opinions buy you.

So Who Should Become a CAP Developer?

The Backend Developer

If you're coming from a traditional ABAP background, CAP will feel both familiar and foreign. The data modeling in CDS? You'll get that immediately — it's an evolution of what you already know. The service definitions? Similar enough to OData exposure patterns. But the runtime is Node.js or Java, and that's where the culture shock hits.

For me, the Node.js runtime was a natural choice. With a background in Java and JavaScript, picking up CAP's event handler patterns and the async-first model felt right at home. There's something satisfying about the lightweight, expressive nature of JavaScript for service development — and the Node.js ecosystem brings a speed of iteration that's hard to beat. If you're an ABAP developer without that background, though, be prepared: suddenly you're in a world of npm install, package.json, and asynchronous programming. The muscle memory you've built over years doesn't quite apply, and that's humbling — but also energizing once it clicks.

But here's what SAP developers bring to the table that's genuinely hard to replace: business understanding. You know what a sales order really is. You know why the pricing procedure matters. You know the fifty edge cases that will surface in production. That knowledge is pure gold in CAP development, and no amount of Node.js expertise can substitute for it.

The Frontend Developer

If you're a frontend developer building Fiori apps or React SPAs, CAP gives you a backend that's actually pleasant to work with — clean, predictable OData V4 services. But understanding why the business logic needs to work a certain way takes time, and that matters more than people think.

The Non-SAP Developer

CAP is built on Node.js and Java — technologies that millions of developers already know. Any competent full-stack developer could pick up CAP and start building. And they absolutely should.

The learning curve isn't the framework itself — it's the domain.

SAP isn't just a database with an API — it's decades of encoded business processes.

A strong Node.js developer will build something that runs, but understanding the process semantics, compliance requirements, and integration patterns takes patience and curiosity. The good news is that CAP's conventions make this ramp-up smoother than it's ever been, and developers who invest in understanding the SAP business context bring a valuable outside perspective.

The Real Answer

The best CAP developers, in my experience, are hybrids. People who understand the SAP business domain deeply and are willing to embrace modern development practices. That's a rare combination today, but it doesn't have to stay rare. It does require intentional investment — this isn't a simple reskilling exercise, it's a fundamental and exciting shift in how we think about SAP development. The good news? People who make this journey become incredibly valuable, because they can speak both languages fluently.

The Side-by-Side Struggle: When the APIs Aren't There

SAP's vision for extensibility is clear: keep the core clean, build extensions side-by-side on BTP, integrate through well-defined APIs. It's a sound architecture — and one that's getting more practical with every release. But it does come with challenges worth discussing openly.

The API Gap

The fundamental promise of side-by-side extension is that you can access the data and functionality you need through released APIs. And SAP has made real progress here — the SAP Business Accelerator Hub keeps growing, and many core business objects now have well-documented, stable APIs.

That said, you will encounter situations where the API you need doesn't exist yet, or doesn't cover the specific aspect of a business object your extension requires. When that happens, you need to get creative. In my experience, teams typically choose between three approaches:

  • Custom API exposure — build a thin wrapper on the S/4HANA side to expose exactly what you need. Fast to implement, but adds to the on-stack footprint you're trying to minimize.
  • Event-based replication — subscribe to business events and maintain a local projection of the data you need. More decoupled, but requires thinking carefully about data freshness and consistency.
  • Data products via SAP Business Data Cloud — when available for your domain, this is increasingly the cleanest path (more on this below).

None of these are one-size-fits-all, and the right choice depends on your latency requirements, data volume, and how close to clean core you need to stay. But they're solvable problems, and with every S/4HANA release, the API surface grows. The direction is clearly right — it's the pace that sometimes challenges project timelines.

The Data Volume Problem

Even when APIs exist, there's another challenge: data volume. Many real-world extensions need historical sales orders, inventory movements, customer interactions — potentially millions of records. APIs are designed for transactional access, not bulk data extraction. That's a reasonable design choice, but it means you need a different strategy.

SAP Business Data Cloud: A Promising Direction

This is where I want to mention something I've been exploring in tutorials but haven't yet used in a real project: SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and its concept of data products.

The idea is compelling. Data products are curated, SAP-managed packages of business data — not just raw tables, but complete datasets with semantics, metadata, and relationships included. SAP handles extraction, harmonization, and delta replication. You activate a data product, and the data flows. For CAP developers, HANA Cloud can now expose these data products as virtual tables, opening a path to consuming vast amounts of business data without building custom replication pipelines.

BDC is still maturing — the data product catalog is growing, and the integration story with CAP continues to evolve. But if it delivers on its promise, it could fundamentally change the side-by-side equation. Instead of each project building its own data plumbing, SAP would provide a managed data layer that treats business data as a first-class product. That's worth keeping a close eye on.

A Realistic Perspective

I believe in the side-by-side model. Clean core is sound engineering, and it's getting more practical with every release. If you're starting a side-by-side project today, budget for integration complexity and be prepared to build a few bridges where the paved road hasn't been laid yet. But the direction is right, and tools like BDC suggest SAP is serious about closing the gaps.

So Is It Heaven?

Not yet. But you can see it from here.

CAP represents something genuinely important in the SAP world: a modern, principled approach to building extensions and applications. It respects developers' time with good conventions. It bridges the gap between SAP's business depth and modern development practices. And with initiatives like SAP Business Data Cloud on the horizon, the pieces are starting to come together.

The journey isn't easy. The competence challenge is real. The integration gaps require patience. But as someone who's built on Java, worked as an architect on Azure, shipped products on AWS, and spent years deep in SAP — I can tell you that CAP is the first thing in the SAP development world that feels like it genuinely belongs in the same conversation as modern cloud-native development.

It's not there yet. But it's heading in the right direction. And that makes me excited to write code again.

I'd love to hear your perspective: where do you see the biggest blocker today — skills, APIs, or data access? And if you've started your own CAP journey, what surprised you most? Let's discuss in the comments.

entry.title: Stop Building from Scratch: Autonomous AI Agents for SAP Sales Order Processing
entry.content:
Why this article?

It is great that we are living in the era of AI technologies where humans are still needed to design and automate 😀. But let’s be honest: give it a few more years and no company will ask "Which AI platform should we choose?" That decision will be made by central AI services based on strict country and corporate regulations. The only thing that will matter is security and reliability.

So, why am I writing this? Because I see so many people struggling with custom, built-from-scratch AI automation solutions at the enterprise level. These projects start quickly, but they get stuck just as fast.

You know, it is like cookies 😀. You can make one in a sandbox; however, to scale that to a corporate production level is a totally different problem.

If you are still wondering why this article matters: I want to highlight that while humans are still designing automation, speed is everything. Deploying autonomous AI agents on a solid, reliable platform is a massive competitive advantage. Those playing around with internal IT projects or relying on System Integrators (SI) to build from zero are being left behind.

Let me explain why using one simple example: Customer Purchase Orders.

The Business Case: The Reality of POs

A company wins a deal (congratulations!), and the Customer sends a Purchase Order (PO).

In an ideal world, this happens via EDI. If you have EDI, you are lucky and you can stop reading. But in the real world, customers send PDFs, scanned images with handwriting, Excel files with macros, or even a screenshot of their laptop screen.

This shouldn't be an issue. Innovative platforms must handle data extraction from unstructured sources out of the box.

“Training? Fine-tuning??”

I know some of you just thought that 😊. Yes, training OCR models used to be mandatory. If you haven't faced the pain of training models, be happy, because you are young enough 😊. Today, this must work without that hassle.

The Main Challenge: Master Data Mapping

Extracting text is easy. The real challenge is my favorite one: Master Data Mapping.

How do we robustly and reliably read the customer’s product description and map it to the correct Master Data Object in our SAP system? And how do we do it without heavy development?

The Solution: SAP Master Data Vectorization

We don't need custom code; we need Vectorization.

Generative AI can write SQL queries, sure. But think about it: how many SQL queries would the AI have to guess to find a material that is semantically similar but written differently? Infinite. Or maybe a few less if you are lucky and the description contains an acronym like "A4 Paper" 😊.

When AI looks for a material master in vectorized data (rather than a database table), it reads by sense, not just by keywords. This allows it to reliably find the right SAP object even if the customer's description is vague.

I know only one SAP-certified Business AI Platform that has this Vectorization feature in its standard set: Skybuffer AI.

 Pic.1 SAP Master Data Vectorization Setup

 Pic.2 Example of Material Master Similarities based Search

The Implementation: Autonomous Agents in Action

This is the essential part: stop playing in the sandbox. Take a platform and create a simple autonomous AI agent.

In this example, I am using Skybuffer AI because it handles the heavy lifting out of the box:

  • Vectorizes SAP master data for semantic search.

  • Reads Emails (including complex attachments and OCR).

  • Safety First: Uses Mailboxes as a staging area (no messy data replication).

  • ABAP Control: It doesn't let the AI "guess" how to create data. It passes structured JSON to an ABAP class/BAPI for safe execution.

  • No Code: configured entirely in SAP Fiori.

The Flow

A Skybuffer AI agent acts effectively as an automated employee. You can schedule it, give it tools, and let it run.

 Pic.3 Scheduling Autonomous AI Agent

Step 1: The Input

The Agent connects to a Mailbox. It reads the email, extracts the attachment data, and places it into the Agent's memory.

 Pic.4 Flow inside the Tool of Skybuffer AI Action Server

Step 2: The Brain (Generative AI)

We connect the Generative AI and give it guidelines.

Note: The vectorized SAP master data is mapped directly to this action.

 Pic.5 Generative AI Action of Skybuffer AI Action Server

The AI creates a JSON structure based on our request. Crucially, we do not ask the AI to update SAP directly. We hand that JSON over to an SAP ABAP class. This ensures the transactional data is created correctly, without hallucinations.

Step 3: The Output

We update the email in the Mailbox with the status and send a confirmation back to the customer.

Everything runs either in your SAP HANA On-Premise shell (directly in your network) or on your SAP BTP tenant.

Conclusion

Autonomous AI agents are no longer "future tech", they are easy solutions available now.

By choosing a Business AI automation platform rather than building from scratch, your company gets the best of Generative AI and the SAP backend immediately.

It is strange that so many companies are still "playing" with in-house development. You wouldn't build your own Sales Order Management system from scratch instead of using SAP, right? 😊 That would be weird. Yet, companies are giving huge budgets to internal teams to build AI wrappers.

You don't need a giant team. You need a reliable solution, speed, and flexibility. Just look at Cursor, they revolutionized coding with roughly 60 employees before their massive 900M funding round 😉.

Be quick. Be autonomous.

entry.title: A Step-by-Step Guide to Integrating SAP SuccessFactors with Power BI through Direct OData Feed
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Why Direct OData Feed? (Comparison of Options)
  • Key Technical Challenges Solved
  • 1. Server-Side Pagination
  • 2. Performance Optimization
  • 3. Row-Level Security (RLS)
  • Implementation Guide
  • Prerequisites
  • Step 1: Create a Blank Query
  • Step 2: The Logic (M Code)
  • Step 3: Publish and Automate
  • Troubleshooting Common Issues
  • Conclusion

Introduction

In the modern enterprise, HR data is no longer just about records—it's about strategic insights. While SAP SuccessFactors is a powerhouse for Human Capital Management, organizations often struggle to visualize this data alongside financial and operational metrics. Many customers turn to Microsoft Power BI for its rich visualization capabilities and ecosystem integration. However, a native connector for SuccessFactors does exist in PowerBI. As a SuccessFactors consultant, I've seen many struggle with this integration due to limited technical resources or budget constraints. This guide provides a lightweight, cost-effective, and powerful solution using the Direct OData Feed method with empowerment of AI. By leveraging this approach, you can build modern, real-time dashboards without investing in expensive middleware or extensive data engineering resources.

Why Direct OData Feed? (Comparison of Options)

There isn't a "one-size-fits-all" solution. The best integration strategy depends on your organization's specific needs, budget, and technical maturity.

   

Integration Option
 Basic IntroductionComplexity Data LatencyBest Use caseLicense RequirementRequired Implementation resoureCost
Direct OData Feed (This Guide)A standardized protocol (OData v2/v4) based on HTTP and REST for consuming SuccessFactors data directly via native Power BI connectors(Advance Query) or Web Service APIs.Low to Medium; requires handling server-side pagination (1,000-row limit) via recursive M-code functions, URI composition, and query optimizations ($filter, $expand). SuccessFactors could complete the task with the power of AIReal-time or Scheduled Refresh; provides methods for CRUD operations directly against the application layer.Small to moderate datasets, simple reports, or scenarios requiring real-time access to logical application objects.No additional license; requires secure authentication (OAuth 2.0/OIDC).Technical expertise in RESTful APIs, Power Query (M) skills for pagination logic, and SF Administrator permissions for API Center and RBP configuration.Low
SAP DatasphereSAP's native cloud data warehouse solution within SAP BTP that creates a unified semantic layer over SuccessFactors data using federation or replication.Moderate to High; involves configuration of cloud-to-cloud connections, semantic modeling, and Graphical Views within the SAP environment.Near real-time via remote tables (federated) or scheduled replication.Organizations requiring cross-SAP module analysis, unified governance, and a centralized semantic layer for SAP and non-SAP data.SAP BTP Credits / Datasphere subscription; Power BI connector for SAP.SAP modeling expertise, Cloud Connector configuration, and Private endpoints for secure access.High
Azure Data FactoryA PaaS-based ETL/ELT service that uses native connectors or the SAP CDC (Change Data Capture) framework to ingest data into a data lake for transformation.High; involves designing multi-stage pipelines (Bronze-Silver-Gold), setting up Self-Hosted Integration Runtime (SHIR), and linked services.Near real-time or low-latency batch using Change Data Capture (CDC) via ODP framework.Large-scale enterprise data warehousing, datasets with millions of records, and complex cross-system data merging.Azure Consumption (pay-as-you-go); SAP .NET Connector (SAP NCo 3.0); may require a trusted third-party IdP for SAML assertions.Data Engineering skills, Windows VM for SHIR, and SAP Basis/Security team for ODP/RFC and Identity Provider (IdP) setup.High
3rd Party Connectors (e.g., CData)A specialized third-party Power BI Connector/Driver featuring a built-in SQL transformation layer that translates SQL queries into real-time OData calls.Low to Medium; provides a plug-and-play setup for OAuth/SAML and automated handling of API complexities like pagination.Live (DirectQuery) or scheduled batch refresh.Rapid deployment of reports without custom coding; real-time dashboards; simplified cloud-to-cloud integration via Connect AI.Third-party CData license (Connect AI or Driver); SuccessFactors API Key/Client Secret.Power BI Desktop; CData Gateway (for Service refresh); SAP Admin for OAuth registration.High

Why we chose Direct OData Feed: It allows for a rapid deployment, requires zero additional licensing, and offers full control over the data query via Power Query (M) language.

Key Technical Challenges Solved

Before we start coding, it’s crucial to understand why this isn't just a simple "Connect" button.

1. Server-Side Pagination

SuccessFactors protects its cloud infrastructure by limiting API responses to 1,000 records per page. If you simply request data, you'll only get the first 1,000 rows. Our solution uses a recursive M-code function to automatically "loop" through all pages until every record is fetched.

2. Performance Optimization

SuccessFactors tables (Entities) are wide. Fetching select * is a recipe for slow reports. We use the $select OData parameter to request only the fields we need, drastically reducing load times.

3. Row-Level Security (RLS)

HR data is sensitive. We can leverage Power BI's USERPRINCIPALNAME() function to match the logged-in viewer with their data, ensuring managers only see their direct reports.

Implementation Guide

Prerequisites

Before proceeding, ensure you have:

  1. SuccessFactors API Key & Client Secret: Recommended to use SAP Blog IAS OIDC Token Authentication as Basic Auth is being deprecated.
  2. API Permissions: The integration user must have permission to query the necessary OData entities (e.g., EmpJob, User).

Step 1: Create a Blank Query

  1. Open Power BI Desktop.
  2. Go to Get Data > Blank Query.
  3. In the Power Query Editor, click Advanced Editor on the Home ribbon.

Step 2: The Logic (M Code)

Paste the following code into the Advanced Editor. This script handles OAuth 2.0 authentication, Pagination, and Data Flattening automatically.

Note: Replace the placeholders (ClientID, CompanyID, PrivateKey) with your actual credentials through "Manager Parameters"

The AI Game-Changer: This step was once the biggest technical hurdle, but AI has completely shifted the paradigm. You no longer need to be an expert in Power Query (M) to succeed. Simply feed the sample code below into an AI tool, specify your SuccessFactors API endpoints and the fields you need, and let the AI generate the code for you. If errors arise, just paste them back into the chat—AI will troubleshoot and refine the script until it runs perfectly.

let
    // --- 1. CONFIGURATION ---
    SF_BaseURL = "https://apiKey.sapsf.cn", // Update to your data center URL
    SF_ClientID = "YOUR_CLIENT_ID",
    SF_CompanyID = "YOUR_COMPANY_ID",
    SF_UserID = "ADMIN_USER",
    SF_PrivateKey = "YOUR_PRIVATE_KEY_ONE_LINE", // Ensure no line breaks

    // --- 2. AUTHENTICATION (OAuth 2.0) ---
    // Step A: Request Assertion
    AssertionResponse = Text.FromBinary(Web.Contents(SF_BaseURL, [
        RelativePath = "/oauth/idp",
        Query = [#"$format" = "json"],
        Content = Text.ToBinary(
            "client_id=" & SF_ClientID & 
            "&user_id=" & SF_UserID & 
            "&token_url=" & SF_BaseURL & "/oauth/token" & 
            "&private_key=" & SF_PrivateKey
        ),
        Headers = [#"Content-Type"="application/x-www-form-urlencoded"]
    ])),
    AssertionID = AssertionResponse,

    // Step B: Exchange Assertion for Access Token
    TokenResponse = Json.Document(Web.Contents(SF_BaseURL, [
        RelativePath = "/oauth/token",
        Content = Text.ToBinary(
            "company_id=" & SF_CompanyID & 
            "&client_id=" & SF_ClientID & 
            "&grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:saml2-bearer" & 
            "&user_id=" & SF_UserID & 
            "&assertion=" & AssertionID
        ),
        Headers = [#"Content-Type"="application/x-www-form-urlencoded"]
    ])),
    AccessToken = TokenResponse[access_token],
    AuthHeader = "Bearer " & AccessToken,

    // --- 3. RECURSIVE DATA FETCHING FUNCTION ---
    GetSFData = (UrlPath as text) =>
        let
            Source = Json.Document(Web.Contents(SF_BaseURL, [
                RelativePath = UrlPath,
                Headers = [Authorization = AuthHeader]
            ])),
            Data = Source[d][results],
            // Check if there is a next page
            NextFullUrl = try Source[d][#"__next"] otherwise null,
            // Extract relative path from absolute URL
            NextPath = if NextFullUrl <> null then Text.Replace(NextFullUrl, SF_BaseURL, "") else null,
            // Recursive call if NextPath exists
            Result = if NextPath <> null then List.Combine({Data, @GetSFData(NextPath)}) else Data
        in
            Result,

    // --- 4. EXECUTION ---
    // Define your OData query here with $select and $filter for performance
    InitialPath = "/odata/v2/EmpJob?$format=json&$filter=emplStatus eq 'A'&$select=userId,businessUnit,jobTitle,jobCode,position,department,managerId,emplStatus&$expand=emplStatusNav&$top=1000",
    
    RawDataList = GetSFData(InitialPath),

    // --- 5. TRANSFORMATION ---
    #"Converted to Table" = Table.FromList(RawDataList, Splitter.SplitByNothing(), null, null, ExtraValues.Error),
    #"Expanded Columns" = Table.ExpandRecordColumn(#"Converted to Table", "Column1", 
        {"userId", "businessUnit", "jobTitle", "jobCode", "position", "department"}, 
        {"UserId", "Business Unit", "Job Title", "Job Code", "Position", "Department"})
in
    #"Expanded Columns"

Step 3: Publish and Automate

  1. Close & Apply to load the data into Power BI.
  2. Build your visualizations (Charts, Tables, KPIs).
  3. Publish to the Power BI Service.
  4. Schedule Refresh:
    • Go to the dataset settings in Power BI Service.
    • Set up a daily refresh schedule (e.g., 6:00 AM) to ensure dashboards always show the latest HR data. Note: The exction are optional because it is usually done by the Power BI Admin. SucccessFactors are mainly responsible for the data extraction and loading into the Power BI Service.

Troubleshooting Common Issues

The "Authorization Header" Paradox

  • Symptom: Power BI throws an error about the Authorization header being disallowed.
  • Cause: Power BI tries to handle authentication natively.
  • Fix: When prompted for credentials for the web source, select Anonymous. The M-code handles the actual authentication via the header manually.And Set the Privacy Level to "Organizational" for all data sources involved.

Data Truncation

  • Symptom: You only see 1,000 rows.
  • Fix: Ensure you are using the recursive function (GetSFData) shown above, not just a simple Web.Contents call.

Conclusion

appreciate all contributors online who write the relevant material about the integration and thank to this AI era. The emergence of AI tools has fundamentally streamlined the integration process. Tasks that previously required weeks of specialized learning in Power Query (M) can now be completed in a fraction of the time. By leveraging resources like Google NotebookLLM for rapid research and Gemini for code generation and debugging, I can shift their focus from writing syntax to orchestrating solutions. This evolution empowers me to deliver complex integrations efficiently, regardless of their initial technical depth in a specific tool.

Finally, I am sharing my NotebookLM notebook on SuccessFactors-PowerBI integration, providing a deep dive into the technical details and research behind this solution. Google NotebookLLM SF-PowerBI Integration

entry.title: SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM - Creating a connection with SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise [SAC]
entry.content:
SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2 (V2) and Enterprise Service Management (ESM) deliver modern, modular customer‑experience capabilities. But as organizations scale, the real differentiator becomes analytics—the ability to unify sales, service, and enterprise operations into a single insights layer.

SAP provides two major analytics options:

  1. Embedded SAP Analytics Cloud (eSAC) – included and built into V2 and ESM
  2. SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise Edition – the full standalone analytics platform

Pre-requisities

  • SSO must be enabled in both systems (SAP Sales & Service Cloud v2 & SAP Analytics Cloud)
  • Login via SSO in Sales and Service Cloud v2 Application.
  • Login to Enterprise SAC using the same SSO as used above.
  • Now try to create the connection and it should be successful (shown in below steps - screenshots).

Note : While doing handshake - Login to both system should be in same browser but in different tabs.

Step 1 : SSO must be enabled in order to work both Applications ( SSC v2 & SAC)

Step 2 : Login to SAP Analytics Cloud (Enterprise License version) and you must be Administrator to config

Step 3 : Select Connect to Live Data - Select a Data Source - SAP Cloud Application

Step 4 : Select from dropdown SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2

Step 5 : Enter the Application URL of your SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2 URL then immediately the Redirection happens to authenticate against both systems if the same IDP matches with successfull response.

 

Conclusion

Integrating SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 + ESM with SAP Analytics Cloud Enterprise unlocks powerful, cross-functional insights. While eSAC provides rapid, embedded visualization, SAC Enterprise enables enterprise-scale reporting and combines data from multiple systems.

For organizations seeking lighter setups, custom integration patterns using embedded Analytics within v2, using V2 REST APIs and BTP or SAP Databricks are viable alternatives.

By following the steps and architecture options outlined in this guide, your organization can create a unified analytics environment that empowers sales, service, and enterprise teams with real-time, actionable insights.

entry.title: Re: Following messages raised while executing migration o...
entry.content:
Good day @Fabiane_Melo74 

Thank you for your question.

The error indicates there are consistency issues between your simulation and actual posting.

FAA_POST 510, in other words currency type 10 and 30 expects historical foreign currency values in your migration file for those currency type. If they are not available or inconsistent, this tends to lead to posing failure even when your simulation looks fine.

Here are references for more details:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/3c0dddd9424549fe9c46db30780c128c.html?locale=en-US

See similar question: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/fixed-assets-postings-migration-error/qaq-p/12411855

AA 481, is typically a follow-on technical error which is triggered by inconsistency in your migration content or checks the system carries out during posting update. This is described in KBA 3367233

Simulation may pas but your posting fails because simulation checks mainly structure and some basic validation. Posting however, performs deeper accounting checks for example currency consistency, update logic and document creation. So, issue in historical currency values often appear during the posting time.

Here is my suggestion:

  • Ensure you fill historical currency amounts for Currency type 10 and 30
  • Ensure your values are consistent across both sheets.
  • Do not mix translated by system logic with historical values provided logic for the same scope.
  • The re-upload and run your posting again.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: How to automate NBP T-1 Exchange Rate logic in S/4HAN...
entry.content:
Good day @Wayne10 

Thank you for your question.

Your approach is good.

The usual cloud compliant solution is to set the relevant billing date filed through released billing extension BAdI, then let standard exchange rate determination read that date. So using SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER for influencing billing data transfer is also confirmed in Custom Data Transfer for Billing Process Documents.

The public cloud recommended pattern to achieve that is to:

  • Use SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER to derive Exchange Rate Date = service date minus 1 working day. Note, not jus minus 1 calendar day.
  • Keep NBP rates loaded with true publication date through data feed.
  • Let standard rate determination pick the rate by the BAdI-derived date.
  • Then finally carry out regression test for normal invoices, credit memos, intercompany billing, holidays, etc.

Your question regarding pitfall:

  • A Pitfall to avoid is implementing plain billing date – 1 day logic. Ensure you implement previous working day logic with company calendar handling, otherwise weekend or holiday invoices will pick wrong dates.    
  • For intercompany, validate which date drives exchange determination in each billing flow. Custom logic may need conditional handling by your billing process.
  • For Credit memo, ensure you confirm whether legal requirement expects original invoice rate or T-1 of credit mem tax point, ensure to apply explicit branching in BAdI.

For Poland T-1 look back behaviour, typically no native config toggle exist. See KBA 3700192 regarding missing standard config. 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: International Editable SALV Day 2026 – Year 18
entry.content:
International Editable SALV Day 2026 – Year 18

 

Dear CL_SALV_TABLE Fans,

Welcome to February 8th, 2026, which is the eighteenth International Editable SALV Day. See below for a link to a blog I wrote to celebrate this day exactly one year ago.

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-members/international-editable-salv-day-2025/ba-p/14011230#M171694

This day marks the 18h anniversary of James Hawthorne going cap in hand to SAP and suggesting maybe the CL_SALV_TABLE could be brought up to functional parity with the CL_GUI_ALV_GRID and have an option to be editable.

https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-discussions/cl-salv-table-needs-editable-functionality-sap-please-provide-this/m-p/3372776

Some popular pop songs are about ABAP features that should have been added 18 years ago – of which there are more than you might think – they often focus on the transition to adulthood, freedom, and nostalgia, featuring tracks like "18" by One Direction and 5 Seconds of Summer, Alice Cooper’s anthem "I'm Eighteen and wonder what a DYNPRO is," and Khalid's "ABAPUNIT". These songs highlight the mix of excitement, uncertainty, and "growing pains" associated with leaving adolescence and having to learn procedural programming and then leave it behind.

I Wish to Table a Motion

The CL_SALV_TABLE was sold as an improvement to the “classic” CL_GUI_ALV_GRID.

SAP is a German company. I lived in Germany for many years. There is a German word “verschlimmbessern” which in English translates to “An Improvement Which Makes Things Worse”. It is good they have a specific word for this. In this specific example the “improvement” was to remove the one thing that made the end users love the CL_GUI_ALV_GRID and replace it with something that made it easier for the developers. But think about it – who is more important to the business? The end users who actually make the money or the developers? Who should you be trying to please?

My whole point, what I have been trying to say all these years, years full of tears, is why cannot one have the best of both worlds? Easy for the end users AND easy for the developers. Is that too much to ask?

Now, way back in 2001, I was at SAP TECHED, and the idea of “embedded analytics” was demonstrated.

The logic being is that one would want to call up a list of sales orders (or whatever) that had errors of some sort, and then fix those errors one by one, all in the same transaction.

I put it to you, ladies and gentlemen of the jury, that this fundamental concept has not changed in the 25 years since this was first shown to me as a prototype.

Just to make this 100% clear, one does not just want to see the incorrect data, one wants to interact with it, in order to correct it.

The current proposition is that only UI5/Fiori can do this, in a screen that looks just like an ALV Grid, only a little bit uglier, and a little bit slower But, it is marketed as a “delightful” experience, so that is all right then.

Now, just to make this exercise even more futile I am posting this on the SAP Community Website – which no-one ever visits anymore.

The SAP GUI is not going anywhere. You may have noticed SAP just extended the life of ECC 6.0 by three more years, albeit with conditions. And even when an organization goes onto S/4HANA often they still use the SAP GUI because people like it. They have moaned about it non-stop for thirty years, but they like it really.

It has been argued that Fiori apps which have a single purpose are the way forward.

Now I have tried these I feel it is a quantum step backwards. One app to set the delivery quantity, one app to set the picking quantity, yet another app to do the goods issue. When it used to be all done in one step in the GUI? Did anyone ever think about this?

In real life end users tend to like SAP GUI applications with million buttons. You can pretend that is not true, but you would be lying to yourself. Take Excel or Word for example and look at how many options they have.

Now, I am just going to repeat what I said last year, word for word.

It is as simple as this.

  • You get asked to write a read-only ALV report.
  • It is faster and simpler to do this using CL_SALV_TABLE than CL_GUI_ALV_GRID because you do not have to mess around with screens and controls and what-not.
  • Indeed, SAP recommend using CL_SALV_TABLE for all new developments of this nature.
  • The report goes live. The users start asking for extra things, as always.
  • They want some custom functions at the top of the screen.
  • They want to be able to change some of the fields and have that replicate to the database.
  • Well, you are right out of luck. You cannot do either with CL_SALV_TABLE.
  • There are workarounds for both, but they are scary, because SAP keeps finding ways to shut those workarounds down, so such an approach is not future proof.
  • The only viable option is to start again, this time using CL_GUI_ALV_GRID.
  • Then the next time you get a request for a “read-only” report the “once bitten twice shy” thing kicks in and you start with CL_GUI_ALV_GRID from the get-go.

I like CL_SALV_TABLE and so do a lot of SAP developers. But everyone (end users) likes having the editability feature a lot more. But it’s never going to happen. As has been mentioned you would think that if 100% of your customers want something you might consider giving it to them, but obviously not.

Very soon – maybe already - we will get to the stage some people reading this blog will be younger than the age of the request to make the SALV editable. Since the SAP GUI is not going anywhere (evidenced by the huge amount of development money pumped into every year by SAP) it is possible I will die of old age before the request is actioned. I am 57 already.

Nevertheless, I will keep going. Miracles can happen.

Until then the situation is “Oi you 31,000 SAP ERP Customers! You see this? You like this? You want this? Well, you can’t have it!”

 

See you next year!

Cheersy Cheers

Paul

PS I must admit I had an AI help me with the pictures this time. Made sense to me, no problems with copyright. I had told it what I was doing, and it said do you want me to write/publish the blogs for you in the next years, and I said no, but when I am dead YES, keep this going forever. I will probably die circa 2040. Will people still be using SAP GUI then? YES!

entry.title: Re: BW-Like Authorizations Multiple-Dim DAC vs Single-Dim DACs
entry.content:
Hi Tobias, as of now you cannot create Dimensions in AMs. So you have to create the "RSINFOCUBE"/PROVIDER dimension in the View. You can have the DACs in AM and link with the "RSINFOCUBE" dimension there. Only drawback is the execute-as, which is possible only in views, not in AMs. 

For the RSINFOCUBE: create a new dimension in each DAC relevant View, simulating 0INFOPROV. Assign PROVIDER_DAC to the  dimension. Since the DACs are separated, implicit AND applies and thus you restrict users for AMs. 

As of now one cannot create dimensions in AMs, only measures.

entry.title: Build custom Joule Agent to analyze blocked freight order in S/4HANA TM
entry.content:
In this blog, the objective is to build a custom Joule agent with multiple skills that analyzes a blocked freight order in the S/4HANA Transportation Management (TM) solution.

To keep the scenario simple, the agent analyzes blocks caused by time conflicts and explains them in simple business language.

What you will learn

By the end of this blog, you will understand:

  • The prerequisites for building this use case
  • How to expose TM documents as OData services consumable by Joule
  • How to create Build Actions, Joule Skills, and a Joule Agent
  • How to write basic instructions for the Joule Agent
  • How to test and fine-tune the agent

All of this can be done with minimal or no coding. With the right access and setup, even a business user can follow these steps and build a basic scenario in about 30 minutes. The approach is similar across S/4HANA solutions, with different OData services used to fetch backend data.

Let’s look at the high-level steps

  1. Pre-requisites
  2. Identify available CDS views in S/4HANA TM and wrap it with an OData service
  3. Configure SAP Cloud Connector – Joule (from public internet) needs secure communication channel to connect your ERP environment
  4. Define Destinations on your BTP subaccount - connect to the OData service via cloud connector
  5. Define Actions in Joule Studio
  6. Define Joule Skills
  7. Define Joule Agent
  8. Test!

Let’s look at the steps in detail.

  1. STEP 1: Pre-requisites

            You need the following to run this scenario,

  • An S/4HANA private or on-premise environment
  • Eclipse IDE with ABAP Development Toolkit (ADT) – see the tutorial if you haven’t done
  • SAP Cloud Connector installed in your landscape – follow this if haven’t done
  • An SAP BTP subaccount with Joule Studio (Joule studio is one of the components of SAP Build) – follow SAP Discovery Center Mission for steps to do that.

STEP 2: Identify available CDS views in S/4HANA TM and wrap it with an OData service

            Look at the SAP documentation for all available standard S/4HANA CDS views. For S/4HANA TM, see here. In this use case, we require data from Freight Order, its item and stop nodes. So, let’s login to S/4HANA system and create an OData service for the three CDS views.

Select a package and create a service definition

 

Select referenced object as the respective CDS view and create and activate the service definition for the three CDS views.

 

Once created the definition, create a service binding by right click on each definition. Follow the wizard and after creation, activate the binding and then publish it. You can see the service URL and note that down. You can as well test the service now.

 

STEP 3: Configure Cloud Connector

Login to your SAP Cloud connector and Configure cloud to On-premise for the subaccount you are going to use for Joule. ‘/’ expose all which is not ideal, but fine for testing environments.

 

STEP 4: Define Destinations on your BTP subaccount

Now login to your BTP sub-account and navigate to ConnectivityDestinations and create a destination of type ‘HTTP,’ proxytype ‘OnPremise’.

The URL must be constructed by combining virtual host URL in cloud connector with service end point. Also add additional properties as shown in the screenshot.

 

STEP 5: Define Build Actions

Now navigate to your Joule Studio (SAP Build) environment.

Navigate to the ConnectorsActions and create an action to fetch freight orders which are blocked.

Once you click create, select OData Destinations as API source. It will display all OData destinations defined in the subaccount. Pick the destination created in step 4.

 

It displays the available services in the underlying CDS view. Select Next and provide a meaningful name and description. Then navigate to the created action project.

 

Initially, the OData service will be with its basic definition. We need to add filter to fetch only filter blocked freight order (just pick execution blocked).

For this, click $filter parameter and then click Value F4 help in the popup. You can use condition editor or Text Expression.

 

Reduce output fields

In the Output tab:

  1. Select the Body parameter.
  2. Remove unnecessary fields.

For this scenario, keep only:

  • Order number
  • Description
  • UUID

You can test the action under the Test tab by selecting the destination and clicking Test.

If destination is not visible, Go to Control Tower view of Build and select Destinations and add the destination you created before.

Do these steps to create three actions to fetch root, item and stop information. Idea here is, first fetch root and then PRD item. From item node, get the FU and go to FU stop to get acceptable and planned dates. Provide all this information to Joule for analysis.

While creating action for item,

  • add a filter to restrict only PRD items.
  • Reduce the Output to have only freightUnitUUID field.

While creating action for stop,

  • Reduce output to pick only location, stoprole, stopcategory,acceptable start and end time and planned time.

STEP 6: Define Joule Skill

Navigate to Build Lobby and click create and select Joule Agent and skill. Once the project is created, click create and select Joule Skill. Provide a clear name and description of the skill.

Click on conversation starter and provide the prompt. Example ‘get execution blocked freight order with stop details and analyze the reason for block’

 

You don’t need any other input from user as we already did filter in our action to pick execution blocked freight orders.

Add the action by clicking the ‘+’ button and select our action to fetch freight order root.

 

Navigate to skill parameter (select anywhere in the editor and click >> button to view skill parameter). Select the output for http 200 code as shown below.

 

Finally bind the skill output to the trigger end of action.

 

Likewise create other skills to fetch item and stop nodes.

For item skill,

  • Skill input must be TransportationOrderUUID
  • Map skill input to action input as shown below

 

  • Finally map skill output to trigger end.

For stop skill,

  • Skill input must be FreightUnitUUID
  • Map skill input to action inputs shown below

  •  

STEP 7 – Create Joule Agent

In the project, create ‘Joule Agent’ and navigate to agent definition screen.

Define ‘Expertise’ which provides the persona of the agent and ‘Instructions’ as system prompt on how to analyze reason for blocked freight order. Write that in plain, simple English. An example as shown below.

 

Provide some few-shot learnings to the agent as part of instructions. Example below

##Example 1 (case with date 'TranspOrdStopPlanTranspDteTme' is before date 'TranspOrdStopAccptblStrtDteTme')

Given the following Freight unit stop details:

- Transportation stop acceptable start date: 10-January-2026

- Transportation stop acceptable end date: 12-January-2026

- Transportation stop planned date: 8-January-2026

// Business Rule: date 'TranspOrdStopPlanTranspDteTme' must be after date 'TranspOrdStopAccptblStrtDteTme'

// Analysis: 8-January-2026 is NOT after 10-January-2026

// Expected blocked freight order Analysis result: Since planned date is not after acceptable start dates, freight order is blocked

Select your desired LLM provider, base and advanced model. Example OpenAI, GPT-4o Mini and GPT-4o. Save the agent definition.

STEP 8: Testing!

Click on the Test icon at the top of the project. Select the actual destinations within BTP to be used at runtime.

The conversation starter will appear and click that.

 

The agent will:

  • Select the required skills
  • Execute them in the appropriate sequence
  • Map parameters between skills
  • Evaluate the data
  • Return a business-friendly analysis using the selected LLM

 

 

Summary:

This example demonstrates how easy it is to build custom Joule agents and skills.
The same approach can be applied to other S/4HANA solutions and a wide range of use cases.

This blog should help anyone new to Joule get started and build a simple proof of concept.
